rationale: The citing paper uses Khan and Dhar (2006) in its hypothesis-development section to support a specific theoretical claim: hedonic choices can be justified when a utilitarian or moral rationale makes indulgence seem more rational. This is not just a bibliography-list mention; it helps motivate why adding attributes could increase hedonic choice by providing a functional justification. The cited work is used conceptually rather than methodologically, and the new paper builds a different antecedent (attribute numerosity) on top of that justification logic.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on Khan and Dhar's licensing/justification insight as conceptual grounding for its argument that cues increasing perceived utility can legitimize indulgent options. It then develops a distinct mechanism, showing that more attributes can serve that justificatory role and shift choices toward hedonic options.
